steps.title: Identify the sentence structure
steps.content: Everything before the blank — "A conceptual artist and designer embraced by both the art world and the fashion world" — is a long noun phrase that describes Mary Ping. The main clause is "Mary Ping was chosen to curate the exhibition."

steps.title: Apply the rule for an opening modifier
steps.content: An introductory descriptive phrase that renames the subject must be set off from the main clause with a single comma. No stronger punctuation is allowed because what follows (Mary Ping was chosen…) is one continuous independent clause that needs its subject.

steps.title: Eliminate each choice
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘world’: no punctuation creates a run-on between the modifier and the main clause. ✗
  • Choice B — ‘world:’: a colon must follow an independent clause, but the modifier is a fragment, not a complete sentence. ✗
  • Choice C — ‘world;’: a semicolon joins two independent clauses; the opening phrase is not independent. ✗
  • Choice D — ‘world,’: a comma correctly sets off the introductory modifier from the main clause. ✓
That confirms D is correct.
